Climate Change and the IPCC According to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), climate change is a long-term, statistically significant variation in a mean climate parameter (such as temperature, precipitation or winds) or in its variability over an extended period (which can last from decades to millions of years).

The increase in greenhouse gas emissions is mainly linked to the burning of fossil fuels and deforestation – both resulting from the population explosion that led to the unbridled use of non-renewable resources.
First, the use of fossil fuels should be replaced by renewable energy sources, such as solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, among others.
Contrary to what many think, this transition will bring cheaper energy to the consumer, and will generate more jobs than the fossil fuel industry.

In addition, it is necessary to protect the vegetation of the planet at all costs – not only with more restricted laws, but also by rejuvenating the area that has been lost.[no sources?] Restoring 15% of the degraded ecosystems of the planet in priority areas could prevent 60% of the extinctions of species expected in the coming decades.
